title: The connection between skin conditions and nervous system dysregulation
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Your skin is talking. Your nervous system is listening.

I spent years staring at my skin in the mirror. Red patches. Flaking. Itching that woke me up at 3 AM. I tried every cream, every diet, every elimination protocol. I spent hundreds on fancy moisturizers. I went to dermatologists who handed me steroid prescriptions and told me to "reduce stress."

Here's the thing. No one asked me about my childhood. No one asked about the knot in my stomach that never went away. No one mentioned that my skin and my nervous system might be having a conversation I couldn't hear.

They're connected. More than most of us realize.

Your skin is the largest organ in your body. It's also the most visible. It's the boundary between you and the world. And when your nervous system is dysregulated, your skin becomes a billboard for what's happening inside.

Think about it. When you're embarrassed, your face flushes. When you're terrified, you go pale. When you're anxious, you might break out in hives or sweat. These aren't random. They're direct signals from your autonomic nervous system, specifically your sympathetic branch, the one responsible for fight or flight.

But here's where it gets complicated. Chronic dysregulation doesn't just cause temporary reactions. It changes how your skin functions at a cellular level. It affects inflammation, immune response, barrier function, and healing. And if you've been stuck in survival mode for years, your skin is going to reflect that.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Not just in the mind, but in the tissues. In the fascia. In the organs. And yes, in the skin. He describes the body as a storage system for unprocessed experiences. When those experiences don't get resolved, they create patterns of tension, inflammation, and dysregulation that show up everywhere.

Your skin is one of those places.

The inflammation loop you didn't ask for

Let me walk you through what happens when your nervous system is chronically dysregulated. Stay with me here, because this is where it gets both fascinating and frustrating.

Your nervous system has two main branches that matter here: the sympathetic (fight or flight) and the parasympathetic (rest and digest). Ideally, they balance each other. You get stressed, you respond, you recover. That's the healthy cycle.

But when you've experienced complex trauma, especially early in life, your nervous system gets stuck. It stays in a state of high alert. Your sympathetic branch is always on, waiting for the next threat. And your parasympathetic branch, the one that calms you down and supports healing, never gets to fully engage.

Here's what that means for your skin. Chronic sympathetic activation increases cortisol, which suppresses your immune system. It also increases inflammatory markers like cytokines. These are the same molecules involved in psoriasis, eczema, acne, and rosacea. They're also involved in autoimmune conditions that affect the skin.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing how trauma reshapes the body. He's found that people with trauma histories have higher rates of inflammatory conditions, including skin disorders. It's not a coincidence. It's a biological pattern.

So you're stuck in this loop. Your nervous system keeps you on edge. That keeps your inflammation levels high. That makes your skin flare. The flare itself becomes a new source of stress, which keeps your nervous system activated. Round and round.

And then the wellness industry tells you to "just reduce stress" and drink more water. Right? As if you hadn't thought of that. As if you weren't already trying.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk lays out the science clearly, but he also offers real pathways for healing. Not just "think positive" nonsense, but actual body-based approaches that address the root.

What your skin is actually saying

I want you to try something. Right now, wherever you are, notice your skin. Feel it. Is it tight? Itchy? Burning? Numb? Is there a specific patch that's bothering you?

Now ask yourself this question: When did this start? Not just the first time you noticed it, but what was happening in your life around that time? Was there a loss? A betrayal? A move? A breakup? A period of intense stress that never seemed to end?

For me, my eczema flared when I was 12. That was the year my parents divorced. That was the year I started walking on eggshells. That was the year my nervous system learned that safety was an illusion. My skin started talking before I had words for what was happening inside.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who specializes in structural dissociation, talks about how different parts of us hold different experiences. Sometimes, a skin condition is the part that's trying to communicate something the rest of you can't say. It's the part that's angry. Or scared. Or grieving. And it's using your skin to get your attention.

I'm not saying every skin condition is caused by trauma. That's reductive and dismissive. Genetics, environment, diet, and other factors all play a role. But I am saying that for many of us, the nervous system piece is the missing link. The one no one told us about. The one that keeps us stuck in treatment cycles that never fully work.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. He walks through how the body stores trauma and how we can gently release it. It's not a quick fix. But it's honest about what healing actually requires.

How to start calming the system

I'm not going to tell you that calming your nervous system will cure your skin condition. I don't know that. And anyone who promises a cure is probably selling something.

But I can tell you what I've seen in my own body and in the bodies of people I've worked with. When you start addressing the nervous system, something shifts. The flares might not disappear completely, but they change. They become less intense. They don't last as long. You recover faster.

Here's what helped me. And I want to be clear: this is not a prescription. This is a starting point. Your body is different from mine. What works for me might not work for you. But these are directions worth exploring.

First, orienting. This is a technique from Somatic Experiencing. You look around your environment and notice things that feel safe. A tree outside your window. The color of a wall. The shape of a lamp. You're not trying to relax. You're just letting your nervous system know that right now, in this moment, there's no immediate threat. Do this for 30 seconds, several times a day. It's small. But it matters.

Second, breath. But not the forced, controlled breathing that feels like another chore. Just notice your breath. Put one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Feel the rise and fall. Don't change it. Just witness it. This activates the vagus nerve, which is the main highway of your parasympathetic nervous system.

Third, movement. But not the kind that feels like punishment. Gentle shaking. Stretching. Walking slowly. The goal is not to burn calories. The goal is to release stored tension from your tissues. Animals do this naturally. Watch a dog after a stressful event. It shakes. That's a nervous system reset. We can do the same thing, though it might look different.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who specializes in polyvagal theory, and this book gives you concrete exercises. It's not theory. It's practice.

And for those nights when your skin is screaming and you can't sleep, a weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure can help downregulate your system. It's not a cure. But it might help you get through the night.

What healing actually looks like

I wish I could give you a neat ending. A tidy resolution. A five-step plan that guarantees clear skin and a regulated nervous system.

But that's not how this works.

Healing is messy. It's two steps forward, one step back. It's having a good week where your skin clears up, and then a bad week where it all comes back, and you wonder if anything you're doing matters.

It matters. Even when it doesn't feel like it.

What I've learned is that the goal isn't to eliminate every skin flare. The goal is to build a relationship with your nervous system that's based on curiosity instead of fear. To stop fighting your body and start listening to it. To recognize that your skin isn't betraying you. It's trying to tell you something.

And when you start listening, the conversation changes. The flares become less about punishment and more about information. You learn what your triggers are, not just the external ones like food or pollen, but the internal ones like certain emotions, certain memories, certain relational patterns.

That's the work. It's slow. It's uncomfortable. It's not linear.

But it's real. And it's the only thing I've found that actually makes a difference.

  • Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana
